rounds.answer_a:
The digital entry for the article "Heroes, Heroines, and History: The History of Matrimonial Bureaus and Dating Agencies" is located on the visual discovery platform Pinterest, known for its "Pin Builder" tool. According to the platform’s interface accessibility text, the specific procedure for reviewing and selecting autocomplete results involves first using the Down Arrow and Up Arrow keys to navigate through the list of suggestions. Once the desired result is highlighted, the user must press the Enter key to select it [1]. For exploration on touch devices, the accessibility guidelines recommend two distinct gestures: swiping left or right with one finger to move between items, and double-tapping with one finger to activate or select an item [2].

## References
[1] https://help.pinterest.com/en/article/accessibility-on-pinterest
[2] https://help.pinterest.com/en/article/accessibility-keyboard-shortcuts-and-gestures
rounds.answer_b:
**Pinterest** is the visual discovery platform utilizing a **'Pin Builder'** tool for creating and customizing pins, though no digital entry for the specific article "'Heroes, Heroines, and History: The History of Matrimonial Bureaus and Dating Agencies'" appears directly in current search results on the platform[1][2]. Accessibility text and interface guidelines on Pinterest emphasize intuitive navigation for search and exploration.

The specific procedure for reviewing and selecting **autocomplete results**, per interface accessibility descriptions, involves typing in the search bar to trigger dynamic suggestions, then using arrow keys to navigate the list and Enter to select a highlighted option. This ensures keyboard compatibility and screen reader support for efficient querying[1][3].

For touch devices, two distinct gestures are recommended for exploration: **long press** (hold finger on a Pin to activate visual search, Lens, or related ideas, now expanded to home feed Pins) and **tap the circle** (in the bottom-right corner of a Pin for Instant Ideas and real-time recommendations). These gestures enable object identification, refinement (e.g., via animated glow or refinement bar for style/occasion), and personalized discovery without complex menus[3][4].

These features enhance accessibility by minimizing cognitive load and supporting diverse input methods, aligning with Pinterest's AI-driven visual search for seamless inspiration-to-action flows[1][2][3].

(Word count: 198)

## References
[1] https://www.frugaltesting.com/blog/the-technology-behind-pinterests-visual-search-and-ai-discovery  
[2] https://passionates.com/pinterest-visual-discovery-social-commerce-giant/  
[3] https://us.fashionnetwork.com/news/Pinterest-boosts-visual-search-via-ai-in-uk-n-america-to-help-womenswear-discovery-and-shopping,1727898.html  
[4] https://www.adweek.com/performance-marketing/kim-davis-chacka-marketing-guest-post-pinterest-visual-discovery-tools/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, regarding Deep Logic, Agent A correctly identified the platform (Pinterest) and sourced its answer from the actual accessibility documentation (`help.pinterest.com`). Agent B failed to locate the specific article mentioned in the prompt and, critically, failed to find the 'interface accessibility text,' instead citing marketing blogs about AI features. 

Second, regarding Width/Completeness, Agent A provided the correct autocomplete procedures (Up/Down arrows, Enter). For the touch gestures, Agent A provided standard screen-reader gestures which align with the context of 'accessibility text,' whereas Agent B hallucinated UI features (Long press, Tap circle) that are unrelated to the specific accessibility instructions requested. Agent B's answer was factually incorrect for the specific constraint.
